channel.title: Version-Control on emsenn.net
channel.description: Recent content in Version-Control on emsenn.net

item.title: Git as Provenance Structure
item.description:
Git is a version control system. It is also, read differently, a provenance structure: an immutable record of what was done, when, by whom, and building on what prior work. This lesson reads git’s data model through that lens, connecting its design to the formal concept of provenance in the Interactive Semioverse.

¶Git’s data model

Git stores four kinds of objects, all content-addressed (identified by cryptographic hash) [@chacon_ProGit_2014]:

item.title: Branching and Merging
item.description:
Branches let you develop changes in parallel. Merging integrates a branch into another branch, resolving conflicts as needed.

Good practice is to keep branches focused and merge them in small, reviewable units.

item.title: Commit Messages
item.description: Commit messages should describe the change and its purpose in a concise form. They help collaborators and future readers understand why a change happened.

item.title: Use Git Basics
item.description:
• Check status with git status before and after changes.
  • Stage relevant files with git add.
  • Commit changes with a descriptive message.
  • Pull before pushing when collaborating.

item.title: What is Git?
item.description:
Git is a distributed version control system that records changes as commits.

It supports branching and merging so multiple lines of work can progress in parallel and be combined later.
